erybody was shooting craps. You know what that is, don't you?

speaker: SS
words: Yes.

speaker: GD
words: Shooting dice. wore down.

words: He told me he remembered them shooting craps where the Greeks lived,

speaker: SS
words: big blankets out on the ground, outside, he said. And after payday came

speaker: SS
words: he said he saw large amounts of money go down on the bets sometimes.

speaker: GD
words: Oh, yeah. There was some gambling. Not large amount of

speaker: GD
words: Probably a hundred or something like that. They

speaker: GD
words: were all gambling. But that was gradually.

speaker: SS
words: Did you ever—?

speaker: GD
words: Gamble?

speaker: GD
words: Yeah. Did you ever lose your paycheck on something like that?

speaker: GD
words: It was popular, everybody was doing that.

speaker: GD
words: Was there mostly crap shooting in Spokane, or was it poker or-?

speaker: GD
words: in the gambling house thereat poker and baccarat and everything else.

speaker: GD
words: It wasn allowed, but they were paying protection, see? The houses were

speaker: GD
words: paying police protection and they don't bother them. But then the pro

speaker: GD
words: secuting :attorney he got wise and they had to cut it out because he come

speaker: GD
words: in and arrested them, see? He took the tables and everything else.

speaker: GD
words: Prosecutor, oh, what the heck was his name? Ican't remember now. I

speaker: GD
words: wasn't in the house. They arrested some of these gamblers, too, you

speaker: GD
words: know. At that time, Iwas out. We weren't there on Saturday nightsnights

speaker: GD
words: and Sundays, because we was working Saturdays, you see.

speaker: GD
words: At that time, we were working six days a week.

speaker: GD
words: Were they mostly lumberjacks or all kinds of people in the gambling?

speaker: GD
words: Oh, everybody, business people, too.. You know everybody was throwing

speaker: GD
words: the dice.

speaker: SS
words: Was that what it was mostly, dice?

speaker: SS
words: Mostly, yeah. 'Course there was some card gamblo. ,but the ln

speaker: SS
words: you know, they could fix the cards so they knew where every card was,you

speaker: SS
words: know,in the deck. See, they were pretty sharp.

speaker: SS
words: Did you guys stay away from the card gambling?

speaker: SS
words: I never play cards, no. I just shot dice.

speaker: SS
words: Could they load the dice, too?

speaker: GD
words: Well, not quite, because they had bone dice. Where they were shooting

speaker: GD
words: dice, they were dice made out of bone, and you couldn't very well load

speaker: GD
words: 'em. Because they were real bone dice and they had cut the corners off

speaker: GD
words: too. They were doing a lot of thingsin Potlatch, too. In Onaway up

speaker: GD
words: there they were bootlegging and gambling and doing everything. Italians

speaker: GD
words: were bootlegging. Not the Greeks, they never did pull it, the bootleg

speaker: GD
words: because they didn't have no places of their own, see. At Onaway, now

speaker: GD
words: one Italian was here and was bootlegging but the others were up to Ona

speaker: GD
words: way. And they were doing it there because it was adifferent town, you

speaker: GD
words: see.

speaker: GD
words: When you say places of their ov~what do you mean? A house to live in

speaker: GD
words: that was out of town?

speaker: GD
words: Yes, yes. Not out of town, at Onaway. You know where Onaway is?

speaker: SS
words: Yes. But not Potlatch.

speaker: SS
words: Well, in Potlatch, too. But not very much in Potlatch. Because they

speaker: SS
words: get arrested here. They had some wine, wine for themselves. And

speaker: SS
words: they used to come from Moscow, you know, the sheriff used to come around

speaker: SS
words: and raid 'em once in a while.

speaker: SS
words: Summerfield?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah, Summerfield. You know him?

speaker: SS
words: I've heard about him.

speaker: SS
words: You don't remember him?

speaker: SS
words: No he's dead.

speaker: SS
words: Been dead for a long time.

speaker: SS
words: But I know Hap Moody, and he was one of that bunch.

speaker: SS
words: Hap Moody is in a home now.

speaker: SS
words: Is he?

speaker: SS
words: Hap is, I know him pretty well. He's a good fella, he pretty smart.

speaker: SS
words: Pretty smart, what you call, sheriff. And, another big fella was sher

speaker: SS
words: iff, he just died here not very long ago.

speaker: SS
words: Jordan?

speaker: GD
words: Jordan.

speaker: SS
words: Well, I've heard about Summerfield stopping a lot of people, but not

speaker: SS
words: about arrests. I don't hear about many arrests.

speaker: SS
words: Oh, no, they didn't. They didn't arrest 'em, they just warned 'em.

speaker: SS
words: Scare him out. And what is this fella we had here/heas around here

speaker: SS
words: once in a while. The last—?

speaker: SS
words: The last sheriff?

speaker: SS
words: No. No, he died, he was sick, you know and he passed away just lately.

speaker: SS
words: Oh, you mean Jordan?

speaker: SS
words: No, not Jordan. After Jordan.

speaker: SS
words: I don't know, it's not Summerfield? It's more recent.

speaker: SS
words: Not Summerfield,recent. Just recently. He used to come over here with

speaker: SS
words: Bob Felton.

speaker: SS
words: Uh huh.

speaker: GD
words: You know now?

speaker: GD
words: Idon't know the name, but Iknow who you mean. Felton is a lawyer.

speaker: GD
words: I think he is prosecuting attorney now, isn't he?

speaker: GD
words: Not— Lara was, but now it's

speaker: GD
words: Larage now, he's out.

speaker: SS
words: Yep.

speaker: GD
words: And he used to fight with them, what you call the commissioners. But

speaker: GD
words: he didn't run. He run the last time, you see. And I think, Felton is

speaker: GD
words: prosecuting attorney. If I remember right, I don't know.

speaker: SS
words: This guy that's prosecuting attorney now he has a beard, I know that.

speaker: SS
words: He's a pretty young fellow.

speaker: GD
words: Is he?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah. The one who's prosecuting attorney for the county.

speaker: GD
words: What is his name?

speaker: SS
words: Ican't think of it. Well, when you say the Greeks-? You say Greeks

speaker: SS
words: didn't bootleg and that was because they didn't have their own homes?

speaker: GD
words: No, they didn't have their own homes. But the Italians

speaker: GD
words: had a- Onaway, too, you see. Mike Petuallo, used to be Onaway, too,

speaker: GD
words: he used bootleg. He was arrested, he went in the jail for couple months.

speaker: GD
words: But he had his own placet family

speaker: GD
words: too, himself, and sold it. No, the Greeks they didn't have anything to

speaker: GD
words: bootleg.Used to bring bottles of beer here from Spokane and he was sel

speaker: GD
words: ling once in a while, but not on a big scale.

speaker: GD
words: Would people buy from the same bottlegger all the time, if they could?

speaker: GD
words: Or would they buy just wherever they could get it?

speaker: GD
words: Wherever they could get it, if they wanted a drink, yeah. They didn't

speaker: GD
words: care who they buy it from. But they knew who was handling

speaker: GD
words: And went over to them.

speaker: GD
words: Wasn't some of it a lot better than others? Iheard some of it was pret

speaker: GD
words: ty bad and some of it was really good, as far as quality, how good it

speaker: GD
words: was.

speaker: GD
words: Oh, quality differed a lot. You mean whiskey?

speaker: GD
words: Yes.

speaker: GD
words: Well, it differ. Some of it good, some of it was no good.

speaker: GD
words: I've heard that in Spokane that they had a lot of houses, too. Prostit

speaker: GD
words: ution there.

speaker: GD
words: Oh, prostitution. It was wide open in Spokane, at one time. But now,

speaker: GD
words: Idon't think they are. 'Course Inever been in Spokane for years. I

speaker: GD
words: go in for business, you know, in and out. I don't know anything about

speaker: GD
words: it because I used to go inKfor the eye go to Spokane. And now Irvin

speaker: GD
words: Anderson took me, his wife , he can't drive himself. He can drive around

speaker: GD
words: town here, but not in Spokane. His wife is good driver. Iused to go

speaker: GD
words: with them. I took quite a few trips for the eye operation.! didnMr vo A.

speaker: GD
words: Where was the place that you gambled? Was that Durkins or was that some

speaker: GD
words: other place? I heard of a place called Durkins.

speaker: GD
words: Durkin was saloon.

speaker: GD
words: Is that where you gambled?

speaker: GD
words: Not only one place, they were wide open. All the saloons places of gam

speaker: GD
words: bling. But they even now— lately they had- they allow gambling in

speaker: GD
words: some places. But I don't think they do now,'course I don't know. I

speaker: GD
words: don't know what they do.

speaker: GD
words: Well, I'm more curious about the early days. When they had prostitution

speaker: GD
words: would the girls just come down in the saloons? Is that where they pick

speaker: GD
words: up the men?

speaker: GD
words: Well, no. They were in certain places.

speaker: SS
words: Houses, yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Yeah, sure. They didn't go down in the street. Oh, there were some down

speaker: SS
words: in the street too, but not too many.

speaker: SS
words: Is it true that they used to come down to this area from Spokane?

speaker: SS
words: Sometime. Sometimes fellas, you know, that knew them, and bring 'em

speaker: SS
words: over here. There was lot of people living here. Now it's dead. They

speaker: SS
words: was running two shifts over there and the machinery took the place of

speaker: SS
words: people, men. And there ' very many men. They used to work a thou

speaker: SS
words: sand or more— fifteen hundred people, you see. They had another street

speaker: SS
words: here, where we lived. Greeks and Italians. They called it the Internat

speaker: SS
words: ional Street. But they took them houses away during the War, because

speaker: SS
words: there nobody to rent 'em, you see. And one fella, he still here, he

speaker: SS
words: took a contract and he hauled 'em away, sold 'em for whatever he can get.

speaker: SS
words: They didn't want to burn 'em down, you see.

speaker: SS
words: What was the working day? Was it a ten hour day, when you were first

speaker: SS
words: here?

speaker: SS
words: Yes, ten hours a day up to when we had that Wobbly strike, whatever they

speaker: SS
words: call that.

speaker: SS
words: The IWW.

speaker: GD
words: IWW.

speaker: SS
words: What did that do?

speaker: GD
words: Well, after that, - that didn't do very much, no, because it didn't last

speaker: GD
words: very long. They all formed the Loyal Legion of Loggers and Lumbermen,

speaker: GD
words: and everybody joined that, you see. And gradually they brought the eight

speaker: GD
words: hour work. And pretty soon— before that, we used to go down there

speaker: GD
words: half hour early to oil the machine. Their's was the machine to .

speaker: GD
words: They had to oil it themselves, and after that, why, they had a man to

speaker: GD
words: oil. We didn't have to do anything except when the whistle blew, you're

speaker: GD
words: ready to go to work, ready to get out, when the whistle at four o'clock.

speaker: GD
words: They had different business. It was strictly houri work. But ten hours—

speaker: GD
words: We work for ten hours then for two dollars a day.

speaker: SS
words: Two dollars a day?

speaker: GD
words: Yeah, I work for two dollars a day here. But they were dollars, see.

speaker: GD
words: Three dollars you could buy a pair of shoes, but now you can't do that.

speaker: GD
words: The dollar was worth something.

speaker: SS
words: With this ten ..hour days did you ever have overtime besides?

speaker: GD
words: Oh, no. No, you couldn't. They didn't have no overtime because they

speaker: GD
words: work ten hours, you couldn't very well have no overtime, work twelve,

speaker: GD
words: thirteen hours. Unless, in some places, you probably work during the

speaker: GD
words: holidays in the cold winter months. When the machinery froze, now

speaker: GD
words: there we had to work half aday in Thanksgiving day and someuLs

speaker: GD
words: in Christmas, to keep the thing running, you see, keep it from freezing.

speaker: GD
words: You mean it would freeze up if it wasn't used?

speaker: GD
words: Yeah, sure, the machinery, yesIK They had conveyors, you know, chains,

speaker: GD
words: and pretty soon if it was frozen— if you start it when it was frozen

speaker: GD
words: it crystalize and it broke. They hadjjdiff erent machinery, you see, old

speaker: GD
words: machinery. They had aburner here where they burn the-'things they

speaker: GD
words: didn't want to use, slabs and things like that. Well they had trouble

speaker: GD
words: with that because it was clear outside, you see, the conveyor in open.

speaker: GD
words: It once in a while it break and they have to fix it. It was different

speaker: GD
words: altogether. And now, it's different altogether down there, even now,

speaker: GD
words: because Ihaven't been down there pretty close to twenty years, you see.

speaker: GD
words: I retired, and I don't know very much about it.

speaker: SS
words: You haven't been down to visit or see what it's like?

speaker: SS
words: Well, I've been down acouple times in the early days, that is, after

speaker: SS
words: I retired. Since then, why, Igo down there and look. Somebody from

speaker: SS
words: Greece came over here and I took him down there to show him, and I

speaker: SS
words: had to go down, but it changed then, you see. But now, there's a

speaker: SS
words: lot of changes. It's unbelievable to me. Where there were three men,

speaker: SS
words: it's only one man and he's got buttons. You been down there in the mill?

speaker: SS
words: I've been at Bennett's but I've never been here at Potlatch, to see

speaker: SS
words: the mill. I've seen the Bennett mill in Princeton. It's smaller but

speaker: SS
words: it's —

speaker: GD
words: That's alright, that's modern mill. That's more modern than this "

speaker: GD
words: because they just built it. Bennett built 'em. Oh, what's his name?

speaker: GD
words: Boone, Boone used to own that, and since Bennett took it over, why, he

speaker: GD
words: remodeled. You know that man there, that head sawyer? He's the only

speaker: GD
words: one, and he punch buttons.

speaker: SS
words: When you worked a ten hour day, did you have to work all ten hours?

speaker: SS
words: Could you take breaks easily, or what?

speaker: SS
words: No. Unless you had to go to toilet or something like that. Otherwise

speaker: SS
words: you work straight ten hours. But they couldn't stop you from that. They

speaker: SS
words: had a man that was cleaning up, you know, and he took your place. He

speaker: SS
words: couldn ttake body's place. It was alittle bit different then. They

speaker: SS
words: had two, three men cleaning up, now they don't have anybody, you see.

speaker: SS
words: The whistle blows and you go to smoke. They allow you ten minutes for

speaker: SS
words: coffee break. That's all. And you have to be there when- you know

speaker: SS
words: there's the whistle blows sometimes.

speaker: SS
words: Uh-huh. During the two o'clock hour- two clock and ten minutes after

speaker: SS
words: two, you'd have to be there.

speaker: SS
words: Well, did they supervise you very closely? Watch what the men did pret

speaker: SS
words: ty close?

speaker: SS
words: Well, not very close. The foreman was a little bit different man- he

speaker: SS
words: watched 'em, yes, because some of the fellas, you know they lay down, nd

speaker: SS
words: they loosen up on their work, they didn't do their work as it should be.

speaker: SS
words: But he couldn't watch everybody. Yeah. Foreman was the whole thing

speaker: SS
words: then. He could fire and hire, but now he can't. The man he want to fire

speaker: SS
words: he goes to the union and complain and they have to put him back or

speaker: SS
words: find out the reason why he is fired. They have different now.

speaker: SS
words: Was that a good way of doing it, for the foreman to fire and hire when

speaker: SS
words: ever he wanted?

speaker: SS
words: Well, it was a good and bad. Because if he— the foreman had a friend

speaker: SS
words: he never fired him,hed fire somebody else. But— you know— he fire

speaker: SS
words: him and put somebody else, that is friendly to the foreman. And that

speaker: SS
words: way is no good, but the Union, I think is better. Although I never did

speaker: SS
words: join the union when I was here. While I was working.

speaker: SS
words: Why not?

speaker: SS
words: Well, there was different things, there was many things- Ijoined the

speaker: SS
words: union when they first come, the AF of L and then came the CIO and they

speaker: SS
words: got the bargaining agency and they wanted me to join that and I

speaker: SS
words: said, "No, Ididn't join that because I joined the AF of Land they lost

speaker: SS
words: out." But, now, of course, it's combined, the AF of Land C0 and every

speaker: SS
words: thing is peaceful. At that time they were fighting one another.

speaker: SS
words: The two, the AF of L and the cfO fought each other, huh?

speaker: DG
words: That's right.

speaker: DG
words: Did you think the AF of L was better? Or did it make much difference?

speaker: DG
words: Oh, well, it's the same thing now.

speaker: DG
words: I mean in those days?

speaker: DG
words: Well, in those days the AF of L came first, you see, and I joined it.

speaker: DG
words: And then they lost their bargaining agency and they wanted me to join

speaker: DG
words: the CIO, and I didn't.

speaker: SS
words: Well, what did you think of the IWW.

speaker: GD
words: Oh, well, the IWWs were radical, anyway. They didn't last long, it

speaker: GD
words: was too radical to exist. They were fighting against the capital, you

speaker: GD
words: see, and you can't do that. Because- in the mill here if IWW strike

speaker: GD
words: they have to break the strike and get somebody to work there, because

speaker: GD
words: they have to run the mill. They have to run the sawmill, you know.

speaker: GD
words: Did you get some of £L talk?

speaker: SS
words: I'm going to, the next time I see him, when I come back to see him.

speaker: SS
words: His wife is a very nice person, too, an interesting person.

speaker: GD
words: Yes, she's—

speaker: SS
words: You say against capital, the IWW— what do you mean?

speaker: GD
words: They were against the companies because they were trying to do the

speaker: GD
words: things that they wanted to do and that was too much. They

speaker: GD
words: they couldn't stand for that. Now, it's different because they agrees to some things.

speaker: GD
words: They don't always agree,

speaker: GD
words: but they have a contract. They sign a contract and they have to abide

speaker: GD
words: by the agreement of the contract. It's different altogether. And at

speaker: GD
words: that time they didn't have no contract. They didn't  nothing.

speaker: GD
words: The IWW organize there first and they want you to— I never did join

speaker: GD
words: the IWW, I don't know very much about it. But, they did try to shut

speaker: GD
words: the mill down in Elk River there and they run 'em out. And they told

speaker: GD
words: 'em they either have to go to work or go to army. That was before World

speaker: GD
words: War I. And some did go to army I know one Greek fella there h e didn't

speaker: GD
words: to either the army or navy. He went to Seattle and change his name and

speaker: GD
words: Kthey couldn't find nothing against him.

speaker: GD
words: Was the only thing he did wrong, was to be a member of the IWW?

speaker: GD
words: Yeah.

speaker: GD
words: Did he do anything else? Try to start a strike? He just joined the

speaker: GD
words: strike?

speaker: GD
words: He wasn't the leader. The leader went in the army. (Chuckles) And

speaker: GD
words: of course, after he come out of the army, he started business in— gamb

speaker: GD
words: ling house in Spokane. And his son, he was general manager of the horse

speaker: GD
words: races, where they got in Spokane. George Manos. But I heard over the radio

speaker: GD
words: the other day where he retired from that position and he's got some

speaker: GD
words: thing else now.

speaker: GD
words: Is the father dead now?

speaker: GD
words: Yeah.

speaker: GD
words: Did you know him?

speaker: GD
words: Oh, yeah,

speaker: GD
words: He believed in the IWW, then.

speaker: GD
words: He was IWW. When the IWW was defunct, there was nothing— and

speaker: GD
words: he didn't care anything about the IWW, because he opened a gambling house

speaker: GD
words: and he other business than working.

speaker: SS
words: What kind of a guy was he?

speaker: SS
words: Oh, he was a good man. Nice fella. But he was a businessman. He was

speaker: SS
words: a friend- pretend to be friend. And he was friend, to a certain extent.

speaker: SS
words: But then, if he could he will take money away from you. You know. He

speaker: SS
words: was pretty good card player.

speaker: SS
words: I thought the IWWs would be against businessmen. Big business, anyway.

speaker: SS
words: At that time, it wasn't—big business, yeah—they were in big business—

speaker: SS
words: you mean in the business that would employ a lot of people9

speaker: SS
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: SS
words: Well, yeah, that's alright. But that place didn't employ very many. It

speaker: SS
words: was just a gambling joint.K The IWW wasn't in existence anyway after the

speaker: SS
words: war. And before the war it went out of existence, it joined the Loyal

speaker: SS
words: Legion of Loggers and Lumbermen.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think what they wanted was good? The IWWs?

speaker: SS
words: They wanted a little bit too much. The company wasn't ready to give it

speaker: SS
words: to them. Now, it's different. Now they sign a contract and they have

speaker: SS
words: to abide by the contract. But it isn't as strong as the IWW.

speaker: SS
words: Was the Loyal Legion of Loggers and Lumbermen, was that a good union?

speaker: SS
words: Oh, well, they— it was mostly for the company. You know, it was to

speaker: SS
words: keep from striking and things like that. The Loggers and Lumbermen

speaker: SS
words: is the predecessor of the AF of L. See, after that was defunct the

speaker: SS
words: AF of L come in and they sign up a lot of people.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think the company would have come around and given the eight hour

speaker: SS
words: day and improved conditions unless the union pushed them?

speaker: GD
words: oh well, I don't think so, no. The company wouldn't do that, but the

speaker: GD
words: unions did do some pushing. The company wouldn't do that, no.N They

speaker: GD
words: wanted the men to work ten hours so they can lumber with cheap

speaker: GD
words: er wages. After they got the eight hour work then they raise their

speaker: GD
words: wages too. To some extent. 'Course now, the AF of L is not as bad as

speaker: GD
words: when they first organized. They was affiliated with the AF of L and

speaker: GD
words: the CIO and that way they worked better.' One is not against the other

speaker: GD
words: and they both working together against the company, the 'employers. Like

speaker: GD
words: the automobile industry and so on.

speaker: GD
words: Well, when you worked ten hours a day, was that very hard to do?

speaker: GD
words: Well, I was young man, I didn't care. It wasn't very hard for me, no.

speaker: GD
words: But now, of course it would be too hard.

speaker: GD
words: I wouldn't like to work ten hours a day now.

speaker: GD
words: Neither would I, but I did. It was ten hours  'over. You work in rail

speaker: GD
words: road ten hours and the railroad was worse than the sawmill, you see.

speaker: GD
words: Pick and shovel and you have to work like the heck.

speaker: SS
words: Like what?

speaker: GD
words: Well, you had to do the job, you see, work pretty hard and the boss was

speaker: GD
words: right there watching you. Yah, the railroad was hard work, at that

speaker: GD
words: time and ten hours a day. And hot. One time we work down to the Snake

speaker: GD
words: River down to Walla Walla and Umatillaand it was pretty hot in summer

speaker: GD
words: time. But at that time I was seventeen, eighteen years old. I didn't

speaker: GD
words: mind it so much.

speaker: SS
words: Did you have any way to get in out of the heat? Was there any place to

speaker: SS
words: go?

speaker: SS
words: But of course, when I came to the sawmill here

speaker: SS
words: after five years after that7K1 thought it was better job here anyway,

speaker: SS
words: and better wages; little better, not very much. Common labor here was

speaker: SS
words: two dollars andrailroad's was dollar seventy-five, dollar and a half,

speaker: SS
words: and harder work. And they shifted from one place to the other, you know.

speaker: SS
words: You told me before that the bosses looked down on the men on the rail

speaker: SS
words: road work. What way did they do that? How did you know that they looked

speaker: SS
words: down?
